course.title: Farenhyt IFP + HFSS Study Guide
course.subtitle: 3-day instructor-led certification prep for keypad programming, HFSS, networking, ECS, and core hardware references

highlights.title: Most testable workflow
points: Know the difference between point, zone, group, and mapping.
Be able to default a panel on pre-V7 and V7+ firmware without mixing the login steps.
Remember that mapping is HFSS-only, while several point edits can still be done from the annunciator.

highlights.title: Lab success focus
points: Always pull the existing configuration before editing when you arrive on site.
Verify the queue shows Complete and 100% after Get or Set Configuration.
Review SLC protocol, point types, zones, groups, and mapping before trying to troubleshoot outputs.

highlights.title: Exam trap reminders
points: Function key macros latch through reset.
Do not mix NACs and relays in the same output group because a group only has one output pattern.

sections.title: IFP keypad programming
sections.summary: This section covers the installer-side keypad work that repeatedly showed up in notes and transcript emphasis: defaulting, password rules, JumpStart behavior, point and zone structure, output grouping, mapping concepts, function keys, and built-in troubleshooting tools.
keynotes: Pre-V7 defaulting: set panel ID, power up, enter installer code 123456, do not press Enter after the code, then go to Panel Programming > Restore Defaults and confirm twice.
V7+ defaulting changes the login path: press Enter at the main screen, select Admin 1, enter the 7-digit Admin 1 code, press Enter, then Restore Defaults and confirm twice.
Restoring defaults clears programming but does not change the firmware version.

Admin 1 on newer firmware must be exactly 7 digits, cannot be the same digit seven times, and cannot be sequential. A model-based code like 0002100 is easier to remember while still meeting the rule set.
Repeated bad attempts trigger lockout behavior and eventually an encrypted hex string that has to be handled through Technical Support and the owner authorization process.
JumpStart is incremental. It adds newly seen devices, removes devices no longer present, and keeps existing programming that is still valid.
JumpStart assigns all inputs to Zone 1 by default and monitor modules default to Manual Pull unless they were already given a different type.
Every input point must belong to exactly one zone. Zones are the logical grouping layer for events and mapping.
Every output point belongs to one output group. Output groups are what you map to, and a group only carries one output pattern.
Do not mix NAC circuits and relays inside the same output group because the group pattern has to make sense for every member.
Mapping is the action/reaction link between input zones and output groups. It can be viewed conceptually from the keypad, but practical mapping edits are done in HFSS.
Function keys are macros. Hold the key, enter the installer code, start recording, perform the disable or enable actions, then hold the same key again to end recording.
Function key macros latch through reset, which is useful for annual testing but easy to forget if you do not document what was left active.
After recording a macro, pull the program into HFSS so the macro exists in the saved digital file. Otherwise a later download can overwrite it.

Built-in troubleshooting tools worth remembering are SLC Device Locator, Multiple Device Locator, I/O Point Control, and Point Status. They help confirm addresses, LED response, basic operation, and detector condition.

flashcards.q: What is the biggest defaulting difference between pre-V7 and V7+ panels?
flashcards.a: Pre-V7 uses installer code 123456 directly with no Enter after the code; V7+ enters through the Login Menu and Admin 1, and you do press Enter after the 7-digit code.

flashcards.q: What does JumpStart do to existing programming?
flashcards.a: It is incremental: it updates the device inventory without wiping the whole program.

flashcards.q: How many zones must an input point belong to?
flashcards.a: Exactly one zone.

flashcards.q: How many output groups can one output point belong to?
flashcards.a: One output group.

flashcards.q: Why should NACs and relays stay in different groups?
flashcards.a: Because each output group only has one pattern, and NAC behavior and relay behavior are not programmed the same way.

flashcards.q: What happens to an F-key macro after a panel reset?
flashcards.a: It remains latched until the key is toggled back off.

examQuestions.q: Which statement about JumpStart is correct?
choices: It erases the entire configuration before scanning.
It is incremental and keypad-only.
It can only be run from HFSS.
It forces every existing monitor module back to Manual Pull.
examQuestions.explanation: Notes and transcript both emphasized that JumpStart is incremental and only available through the panel/annunciator side, not through HFSS.

examQuestions.q: What is the safest way to describe the relationship between zones and groups?
choices: Zones are outputs and groups are inputs.
Zones collect inputs; groups collect outputs.
Zones and groups are interchangeable.
Groups only exist on networked systems.
examQuestions.explanation: Input points are assigned into zones, while output points are assigned into groups. Mapping is what ties zones to groups.

examQuestions.q: A tech records a function key macro and then immediately leaves site without a Get Configuration. What is the risk?
choices: The macro becomes read-only.
The macro will only work once.
A later Set Configuration can erase the macro from the saved file.
The panel will reject the next reset.
examQuestions.explanation: The course repeatedly stressed pulling the current configuration after recording macros so the digital file matches the live panel.

workflows.title: Default a V7+ panel
steps: Set the panel ID appropriately before power-up.
Press Enter at the main screen and choose Admin 1.
Enter the Admin 1 code, then press Enter.
Choose Restore Defaults and change the warning from No to Yes.
Press Enter twice to confirm and save the programming change.

workflows.title: Record a function key macro
steps: Hold the desired function key.
Enter the installer code and start F-key recording.
Perform the disable or enable actions you want captured.
Back out to the main menu and hold the same function key again to end recording.
Pull the configuration into HFSS so the saved file includes the macro.

labChecklist: Confirm the panel firmware family before using the wrong defaulting/login path.
Verify SLC protocol before running JumpStart on a new or changed system.
Check that monitor modules have the correct type after JumpStart, because default Manual Pull can be wrong for the job.
Document function key purpose and current state before leaving site.
commonMistakes: Pressing Enter after the code on a pre-V7 panel, or forgetting Enter on a V7+ panel.
Assuming JumpStart is a clean wipe instead of an incremental update.
Leaving all inputs in Zone 1 and forgetting to reorganize them into useful logical groups.
Mixing output device types in one group and then wondering why pattern behavior is wrong.
troubleshooting: SLC Device Locator can help identify the right address in the field and times out after about 10 minutes.
Multiple Device Locator helps catch duplicate address situations because multiple devices flash together.
Point Status helps distinguish normal, alarm, maintenance alert, and calibration trouble states.
quickReference: Fixed SBUS IDs repeatedly referenced in training: 97 = internal SLC, 98 = internal power supply, 99 = onboard annunciator.
Group 1 is the default catch-all output group after JumpStart; 998 and 999 are reserved defaults for onboard relays.
Pattern 00 = constant; pattern 19 = System Sensor sync, which matters when reviewing NAC programming examples.

terms.definition: One addressable initiating device such as a detector or module.
A logical group of input points used for event organization and mapping.
A single controlled output such as a NAC, relay, or other output circuit.
A logical group of output points that share activation behavior and are used in mapping.
A keypad-driven auto-programming process that scans the SLC and updates the system incrementally.

sources: Instructor Led Sections/THE BASICS OF PROGRAMMING/SELF NOTES/*.txt
Instructor Led Sections/THE BASICS OF PROGRAMMING/Transcripts/*.txt
Instructor Led Sections/THE BASICS OF PROGRAMMING/Covered Slides/3-IFP Programming Presentation - V2.pdf

sections.title: HFSS software workflow
sections.summary: HFSS is where the class moved from keypad concepts into full configuration management: opening programs, licensing, communications, queue verification, point and group programming, mapping detail, password changes, reports, and templates.
keynotes: The class emphasized simple workstation requirements: 64-bit Windows 10 or 11 and at least one active USB port.
Best practice is to keep one program open at a time so you do not edit the wrong panel file.
HFSS licensing for panel programming is tied to CLSS licensing and the user account needs the proper training/license rights.
The green status bar message the class treated as the connection-ready indicator was 'Waiting on USB port.'
Get Configuration means upload from panel to computer; Set Configuration means download from computer to panel.
Do not trust the transfer until the queue shows Complete and the progress bar is at 100%.
Active supervisory conditions can block communication, so do not assume USB alone is the problem when the panel will not talk.
Password changes from HFSS require both a new enough HFSS version and a new enough panel firmware version.
Detector status reporting is useful because it shows normal, maintenance alert, and calibration trouble values across detectors and can be exported.
Event history can be pulled later and filtered, which makes it useful for after-the-fact troubleshooting.
Zone event mapping options such as latching, silenceable, timers, and map inhibit matter because they directly change output behavior.
The notes warned not to use both latching and silenceable together on the same mapping the way some beginners try to do. Treat those settings deliberately.
Templates in HFSS act like a group of groups: they let you map or disable multiple output groups together more efficiently.
Removing zone mapping is not an undo-friendly cleanup step. Treat it like a destructive action.

flashcards.q: What does Get Configuration mean?
flashcards.a: Upload the live panel program from the panel to your computer.

flashcards.q: What two signs confirm a good queue transfer?
flashcards.a: Status shows Complete and the progress bar reaches 100%.

flashcards.q: What simple workstation practice reduces programming mistakes?
flashcards.a: Keep only one HFSS program open at a time.

flashcards.q: What report helps review detector maintenance condition?
flashcards.a: Detector Status Reports.

flashcards.q: What is an HFSS template in plain language?
flashcards.a: A grouped collection of output groups that can be mapped or disabled together.

flashcards.q: Why should you be careful with Remove Zone Mapping?
flashcards.a: Because it is treated like a destructive delete, not a temporary hide.

examQuestions.q: Which statement about HFSS communication is correct?
choices: Get Configuration downloads from the cloud.
Set Configuration uploads from the panel to the laptop.
Get Configuration uploads from the panel to the computer.
Neither Get nor Set changes the queue.
examQuestions.explanation: The course used 'upload' for panel-to-computer Get Configuration and 'download' for computer-to-panel Set Configuration.

examQuestions.q: What should you verify before trusting a finished transfer?
choices: That the file icon changed colors.
That the queue shows Complete and 100%.
That the Windows USB sound played.
That the panel returned to normal.
examQuestions.explanation: The queue confirmation was repeated as the real proof that the action completed successfully.

examQuestions.q: Why is keeping only one HFSS program open a recommended habit?
choices: HFSS cannot save two files at once.
It reduces the chance of editing the wrong panel configuration.
Licensing only permits one file per day.
Templates only work in single-window mode.
examQuestions.explanation: The training framed it as human-error prevention rather than a software limitation.

workflows.title: Safe HFSS connection flow
steps: Confirm laptop requirements and that the USB port is enabled.
Open only the one panel file you intend to work on.
Connect and verify the status bar indicates the panel is ready.
Run Get Configuration before editing so you start from the live program.
Watch the queue until it is Complete and 100% before moving on.

workflows.title: Review detector condition in HFSS
steps: Open the detector status report for the connected or saved configuration.
Sort for maintenance alert or calibration trouble conditions.
Export the report if you need a field handoff or service follow-up list.

labChecklist: Check the queue after every Get or Set.
Pull event history and detector reports when troubleshooting instead of relying on memory alone.
Review map options carefully when outputs behave differently than expected.
commonMistakes: Working from an outdated file because the current panel config was never pulled first.
Assuming USB is the only communication issue when the panel actually has an active condition blocking the session.
Changing mapping options casually without understanding latching, silenceable, timers, and inhibit settings.
troubleshooting: Use event history filters to isolate recent alarm, supervisory, or trouble behavior.
Use detector status reports to distinguish maintenance trending from actual replacement-level calibration trouble.
When a mapping behaves strangely, review inhibit, latching, silenceability, and timer options rather than only checking the zone/group link itself.
quickReference: HFSS version 8.0.21.10 or later plus panel firmware 7.00.16 or later were the course thresholds for changing panel passwords through software.
Licensing lives under the Help menu and ties into CLSS credentials/permissions.
Templates are useful when one zone needs to drive many output groups consistently.

terms.definition: Transfer the current panel program from the panel into the computer file.
Transfer the edited computer file into the panel.
An HFSS report showing detector values and statuses such as normal, maintenance alert, and calibration trouble.
A reusable grouping of output groups that simplifies mapping or disable workflows.

sources: Instructor Led Sections/HFSS SOFTWARE/Covered Slides/4-HFSS Presentation.pdf

sections.title: Networking and multi-panel operation
sections.summary: Networking in this course meant panel-to-panel networking, not general IT networking. The repeated ideas were SK-NIC hardware, unique panel IDs, same firmware families, site behavior, and the difference between networked cooperation and independent site operation.
keynotes: Networking in the course means Farenhyt panel-to-panel communication, not ordinary IPv4 office networking.
Every panel in a multi-panel site needs an SK-NIC network interface card.
Up to 32 panels can be in the network, and each one must have a unique panel ID.
IFP-2100, RFP-2100, IFP-300, and IFP-75 can be part of the same network family according to the course slides.
The RFP-2100 provides the power and point capacity of the 2100 platform without the onboard user interface, which is why it shows up as an expansion option in networked systems.
Networked panels can share a larger system while still being organized into sites so events from one site are isolated from another when required.
The notes called out that ACK, DRILL, RESET, and F-macro keys are disabled until a multi-site user logs in and selects a site.
Copper or fiber can be used through the SK-NIC depending on the hardware path, with the notes specifically calling out twisted pair and fiber optic variants.
A to A and B to B matters when wiring the copper path between NIC ports.
Networking work should start with powered-down panels, correct panel IDs, NIC installation, wiring, then panel power-up and Learn Network/member configuration in the menu flow.
Matching firmware across networked panels matters. Do not treat that as optional.

flashcards.q: What hardware is required to network Farenhyt panels together?
flashcards.a: An SK-NIC in each participating panel.

flashcards.q: How many panels can be in one default network?
flashcards.a: Up to 32 panels.

flashcards.q: What is the easiest way to describe the RFP-2100?
flashcards.a: A network-capable 2100-class panel without the built-in user interface.

flashcards.q: What does the course mean by networking?
flashcards.a: Panel-to-panel Farenhyt networking, not normal office IP networking.

examQuestions.q: Which statement is correct for a Farenhyt panel network?
choices: Only one panel needs an SK-NIC.
Each panel must have a unique panel ID.
All panels must be the exact same model.
Networking requires ECS hardware.
examQuestions.explanation: The course slides and notes both stressed unique panel IDs on every panel in the network.

examQuestions.q: What is the most accurate description of RFP-2100 in a networked system?
choices: A non-addressable repeater panel.
A 2100 platform without a display/interface used to add capacity.
A software-only virtual panel.
A dedicated voice controller.
examQuestions.explanation: The networking material described RFP-2100 as providing 2100-class capacity without an onboard user interface.

workflows.title: Basic panel networking sequence
steps: Power down all panels being networked.
Set each panel ID appropriately and install the SK-NIC hardware.
Wire the network path correctly between panels or media converters.
Power up the panels and verify firmware compatibility.
Use the network programming flow to learn the network and adjust member/site settings as needed.

labChecklist: Verify unique IDs before trying to learn the network.
Verify same firmware family on all networked panels.
Confirm the chosen media path: twisted pair, multi-mode fiber, or single-mode fiber as applicable.
After hardware changes, review the network programming menu and then re-pull the configuration.
commonMistakes: Thinking network means general IT networking and overlooking the SK-NIC requirement.
Reusing a panel ID that already exists elsewhere on the network.
Forgetting that some user actions are site-dependent until the operator selects the active site.
troubleshooting: Start with IDs, firmware match, NIC installation, and wiring polarity before assuming the software is the problem.
If a site behaves independently, confirm whether that is the intended site isolation design rather than a failure.
quickReference: Up to 32 panels can be networked in the default design described in the slides and manuals.
SK-NIC is the required network interface card for these panels.
A multi-site annunciator can be used when events from separate sites still need to be viewed centrally.

terms.definition: The Farenhyt network interface card used to network compatible panels.
A logical partition inside a network that can isolate events and operator control behavior.
A 2100-class panel variant used to add networked point/power capacity without a built-in display.

sections.title: ECS and voice platform overview
sections.summary: The provided material had very little note-level ECS commentary, so this section leans more heavily on the covered manuals and datasheets. It is framed as a product and workflow reference rather than a step-by-step field programming guide.
keynotes: IFP-2100ECS and IFP-300ECS are addressable fire alarm control panels with ECS capability when the emergency communication features are enabled.
The 2100ECS platform supports larger point counts and higher ECS expansion capacity than the 300ECS platform, which is the smaller-capacity option.
Both ECS-capable panels can be networked using SK-NIC hardware and are described with support for up to 32 sites/panels in their product literature.
The 2100ECS documentation references support for combinations of ECS-50W, ECS-125W, ECS-INT50W, and ECS-DUAL50W amplifiers for a much larger overall system wattage ceiling.
The 300ECS documentation references the same amplifier family but at a smaller overall expansion scale than the 2100ECS.
The product references repeatedly name ECS-NVCM as the Network Voice Control Module and ECS-LOC as the Local Operator Console family.
ECS product selection matters because system scale, speaker circuit count, amplifier quantity, and voice/network architecture differ between models.
Use the ECS manuals for exact installation, amplifier, speaker, and module wiring decisions rather than relying on memory from class.

flashcards.q: Which ECS panel is the higher-capacity platform in this course material?
flashcards.a: IFP-2100ECS.

flashcards.q: What does ECS-NVCM stand for?
flashcards.a: Network Voice Control Module.

flashcards.q: What does ECS-LOC stand for?
flashcards.a: Local Operator Console.

flashcards.q: Why should ECS model choice be reviewed early?
flashcards.a: Because the panel platform affects point capacity, amplifier scale, and voice expansion options.

examQuestions.q: Which source should be treated as the best authority for exact ECS installation and wiring detail?
choices: Transcript snippets alone
A remembered classroom demo
The ECS and panel installation manuals
Only the quick-reference study cards
examQuestions.explanation: The ECS section in this project is intentionally more cautious because the available high-signal notes were limited; the manuals are the right final authority for exact details.

workflows.title: ECS planning questions before programming
steps: Confirm whether the job is on 2100ECS or 300ECS.
Confirm the required amplifier family and total speaker load.
Confirm whether network voice control or local operator consoles are part of the design.
Use the installation manual for the exact hardware and wiring path before field programming.

labChecklist: Confirm the panel family before selecting ECS hardware assumptions.
Cross-check amplifier and speaker circuit limits in the manual, not from memory.
Check whether the job includes ECS-NVCM, ECS-RVM, or ECS-LOC-related devices.
commonMistakes: Treating 2100ECS and 300ECS as interchangeable when their overall scale is different.
Using voice hardware names loosely instead of confirming the exact module family in the design documents.
troubleshooting: If ECS behavior seems inconsistent, verify the exact amplifier and voice-module family in the program and hardware bill first.
When in doubt, return to the panel-specific ECS manual because the installation details are model dependent.
quickReference: Amplifier families repeatedly referenced: ECS-50W, ECS-125W, ECS-INT50W, ECS-DUAL50W.

terms.definition: Emergency Communication System capability integrated with the Farenhyt fire platform.
Network Voice Control Module used in ECS architectures.
Local Operator Console used with ECS systems.

sections.title: Panel and hardware quick reference
sections.summary: This section condenses the most useful product-level references that appeared in datasheets and manuals so the search experience has structured product facts instead of relying only on visible HTML or memory.
keynotes: The IFP-2100 family starts with one built-in SLC and can scale much higher in total point capacity than the IFP-300 family.
The 2100 family includes eight onboard Flexput circuits that can be configured for auxiliary power, notification outputs, or conventional detector inputs.
Both IFP-2100 and IFP-300 literature reference built-in communication options such as dual phone line DACT with IP or optional cellular paths depending on the configuration.
The 6815 expands SLC capacity for IDP/SK devices on supported panels.
The 5815XL is the SD-device-oriented SLC expansion path mentioned in the panel datasheets.
RA-2000 is repeatedly referenced as a recommended annunciator option in the course notes and product family literature.
SK-NIC is the network card you need for multi-panel interconnection.

flashcards.q: What is the purpose of the 6815?
flashcards.a: It is an SLC expander for supported Farenhyt panels using IDP/SK devices.

flashcards.q: What hardware handles panel networking?
flashcards.a: SK-NIC.

flashcards.q: What type of device is RA-2000?
flashcards.a: A remote annunciator used with the panel family.

examQuestions.q: Which item is an SLC expansion product rather than a network card?
choices: SK-NIC
ECS-NVCM
examQuestions.explanation: 6815 is the SLC expansion board/product; SK-NIC is the network interface card.

workflows.title: Quick hardware identification
steps: Identify the base panel family first: 2100, 300, or ECS variant.
Identify whether the accessory is adding SLC capacity, annunciation, networking, or ECS voice capability.
Use the datasheet/manual for exact compatibility and capacity confirmation.

labChecklist: Confirm whether the SLC expansion path is 6815 or 5815XL based on device family.
Confirm whether the job needs RA-series annunciation or full network visibility.
Confirm whether networking requires SK-NIC or whether the panel is standalone.
commonMistakes: Confusing SLC expansion hardware with networking hardware.
Assuming model families share the same point ceilings without checking the datasheet.
troubleshooting: When a hardware assumption looks wrong in software, verify the exact model number first.
Use datasheet model names when talking to support so point-capacity and compatibility discussions stay precise.
quickReference: 6815 = SLC expander for IDP/SK devices on supported panels.
5815XL = SLC expander path for SD devices.
RA-2000 = remote annunciator family reference from course material.

terms.definition: An SLC expander used to increase addressable capacity for IDP/SK device loops on supported panels.
An SLC expander path used for SD device support on supported panels.
A remote annunciator option referenced in the Farenhyt family.

sections.title: Certification prep and study strategy
sections.summary: This is the study-focused layer drawn from repeated instructor emphasis and the project brief: prepare for practical labs and for a separate multiple-choice final without pretending every feature is equally likely to be tested.
keynotes: The instructor repeatedly emphasized mastering the basics first: points, zones, groups, mapping, and safe program transfer workflow.
For lab success, think in action-reaction chains: device type -> point -> zone -> mapping -> output group -> pattern/behavior.
For the exam, expect questions that test differences between similar ideas, such as keypad vs HFSS capability or pre-V7 vs V7+ behavior.
Many product names matter less than understanding what role each item plays in the system.
The safest test strategy is to eliminate answers that confuse zones with groups, networking with SLC expansion, or upload with download.

flashcards.q: What concept chain should you be able to explain for lab prep?
flashcards.a: Point -> zone -> mapping -> output group -> output behavior.

flashcards.q: What kind of difference is frequently testable?
flashcards.a: Two similar workflows that differ in one key step, such as pre-V7 vs V7+ defaulting.

flashcards.q: What is the safest answer pattern to avoid?
flashcards.a: Any answer that swaps inputs and outputs or swaps upload and download language.

examQuestions.q: What study approach best matches the instructor guidance?
choices: Memorize obscure options first.
Master the basics before the niche settings.
Ignore mapping because it is too advanced.
Focus only on datasheet numbers.
examQuestions.explanation: The instructor directly advised students to lock in the basic workflows first and treat advanced/special-use settings as secondary.

workflows.title: Night-before review plan
steps: Review the overview cards and top quick-reference items.
Drill flashcards for keypad, HFSS, and networking differences.
Work the practice questions and read the explanations, especially on wrong answers.
Use deep search for any term or model you still cannot place confidently.

labChecklist: Know what you can do at the annunciator versus what requires HFSS.
Be able to explain a clean upload/download workflow without mixing the terms.
Know the common product roles: panel, annunciator, NIC, SLC expander, ECS modules.
commonMistakes: Trying to memorize only isolated facts without understanding the system flow.
Ignoring explanations on missed practice questions instead of learning the pattern behind the mistake.
troubleshooting: If you miss several questions on one topic, use search to pull both clean study notes and deep source excerpts for that concept.
Rehearse the exact order of key workflows out loud because many errors come from swapping two adjacent steps.
quickReference: Most exam-like topics in this project: defaulting, password rules, JumpStart, zones vs groups, HFSS transfer direction, SK-NIC networking basics.
Most lab-critical habits in this project: pull current config first, verify queue, document macros, verify mapping behavior intentionally.

terms.definition: Search mode that looks into the broader source-text dataset, including transcripts and manual excerpts.
Search mode that prioritizes cleaned study-guide content from data.json.

sources: Instructor emphasis captured in programming and HFSS transcripts
